comment.body:
so negative charm convexity means here that there was shorter charm above and longer charm below, this is why theere was no delta decay from charm below my marked "charm flip" line.
But charm convexity is the same as gamma to time.
Logically: 
premium vs spot = delta
(premium vs spot) vs time = charm
(premium vs spot) vs spot = gamma
((premium vs spot) vs spot) vs time = ((premium vs spot) vs time) vs spot = color

so the gamma profile net lowered that naturally increased the volatility, but we were in a negative speed environment = shorter upside gamma, longer downside gamma.
also negative vanna. so vol up/delta down = MMs are buying, but there is shorter delta upside so less resistance, that kinda gamma squeeze-ish. 
This is why. The lvl mattered alone. That was the kinda line in the sand

comment.body:
I take longer-term trades only when the trend is clear on that timeframe, and one can rationally expect lower fluctuations. 
Depends on the direction (short/long), and on the characteristics of the expected movement, bcs one should adjust the vega and theta profile to it as well. 
Like if you are expecting a melt up like last year, with a longer spot/vol beta, you don't buy a calendar spread bcs of the vomma risk. Just an example. I will cover this later in posts

comment.body:
very much. 
lower correlation = low vol = risk exposure can be diversified. they kinda offset each other
higher correlation = higher vol = higher volume hedging activity, higher costs, slippage, strain market liquidity, and the multi-asset portfolio's cross-greek exposures spike basically, so they need to rebalance their books pretty fast, reflexively increasing volatility. Also they use correlation swaps to offset these kind of risks.

comment.raw_body: Dear Alma, how does correlation affect the dealer hedging activity? Also it seems like no interest rate factor being input for the dealer flow model, is that bcs the yield has little impact on short term dealer hedge??

user.name: Jw
user.bio: Jw

comment.body:
risk aversion (bias towards downside protection), dividents, borrowing costs, supply/demand imbalances, early exercise features on american options, bid/ask spreads and transaction costs... 
parity is an ideal, reality is different.

Black-Scholes is an idealistic formula with bad assumptions. It is adjusted at many lvls, i.e. the smile (not to mention the smirk) doesn't follow from BS.
comment.raw_body:
risk aversion (bias towards downside protection), dividents, borrowing costs, supply/demand imbalances, early exercise features on american options, bid/ask spreads and transaction costs... 
parity is an ideal, reality is different.

Black-Scholes is an idealistic formula with bad assumptions. It is adjusted at many lvls, i.e. the smile (not to mention the smirk) doesn't follow from BS.

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
Hello Alma,

Many times you have stated over the past 2 weeks that “calls have underpriced odds”. How can this be possible when “put/call parity” exists? Aren’t puts/calls always equal in price relative to where spot is on the underlying asset? Thanks!
